sentence: Subjective:
Randy Gutierrez, a 2-year-old male, presented with symptoms that started around suppertime last night.
He has a runny nose, stuffy nose, clear mucus, cough, and has been pulling on his ears.
He has also been rubbing his nose excessively.
His rectal temperature was 100.3.
He ate his dinner last night but barely ate his breakfast this morning.
He has been irritable and has difficulty sleeping.
His energy level is variable.
His older sister has been sick, and he had a date with his nephew who has a sinus infection.
He has been treated with saline mist, Tylenol, baby Vicks, and a humidifier.
His cradle cap has worsened.
Objective:
Vital signs: rectal temperature 100.3, pulse rate [NOT MENTIONED], blood pressure [NOT MENTIONED], oxygen saturation [NOT MENTIONED], and temperature [NOT MENTIONED].
Physical exam: The examination of the ears was normal, and the cradle cap was present.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 2-year-old male with a viral upper respiratory infection.
Problem list: Upper respiratory infection; cradle cap.
Plan:
I recommended keeping up with fluids and rest and watching for a barking cough.
If a barking cough develops, I instructed the parents to give a half a teaspoon of the sister's medication.
I also suggested using Zarbee's for the stuffy nose and Zarbee's for kids under a year of age.
The parents were instructed to use a quarter teaspoon of Zarbee's.
I advised against giving honey to the patient due to the risk of botulism spores.
I also recommended using a gentle shampoo, such as Head and Shoulders or Selsun Blue, to treat the cradle cap, and using the cradle cap brush to remove dead scales.
Follow-up:
The patient is to return in one week for a follow-up appointment.
